   },
   "source": [
    "***Morphology - Counting objects***\n",
    "\n",
    "Morphology (or mathematical morphology) is a framework and a collection of image processing methods for measuring and analyzing basic shapes. Morphology is usually applied to binary images but can be used with grayscale also. A binary image is an image in which each pixel takes only two values, usually $0$ and $1$. Binary images are often the result of thresholding an image, for example with the intention of counting objects or measuring their size. A good summary of morphology and how it works is in http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Mathematical_morphology.\n",
    "   \n",
    "Morphological operations are included in the scipy.ndimage module morphology. Counting and measurement functions for binary images are in the scipy.ndimage module measurements. Let’s look at a simple example of how to use them. We start with counting the objects in the binary 'houses.png':"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "execution_count": 1,
   "metadata": {},
   "outputs": [
    {
     "name": "stdout",
     "output_type": "stream",
     "text": [
      "Number of objects: 45\n"
     ]
    },
    {
     "data": {
      "image/png": "\n",
      "text/plain": [
       "<Figure size 720x720 with 2 Axes>"
      ]
     },
     "metadata": {
      "needs_background": "light"
     },
     "output_type": "display_data"
    }
   ],
   "source": [
    "import matplotlib.pyplot as plt\n",
    "from PIL import Image\n",
    "from numpy import *\n",
    "from scipy.ndimage import label,morphology,binary_opening\n",
    "# load image and threshold to make sure it is binary\n",
    "im = array(Image.open('houses.png').convert('L'))\n",
    "im = 1*(im < 128)\n",
    "labels, nbr_objects = label(im)\n",
    "print(\"Number of objects:\", nbr_objects)\n",
    "# Figure\n",
    "fig, axs = plt.subplots(1, 2,figsize=(10,10))\n",
    "axs[0].imshow(im,cmap='gray')\n",
    "axs[0].set_title('Original Image', size=16)\n",
    "axs[1].imshow(labels,cmap='gray')\n",
    "axs[1].set_title('Labels array', size=16)\n",
    "plt.tight_layout()"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "This loads the image and makes sure it is binary by thresholding.Multiplying by 1 converts the boolean array to a binary one. Then the function label() finds the individual objects and assigns integer labels to pixels according to which object they belong to. The figure shows the labels array. The graylevel values indicate object index. As you can see, there are small connections between some of the objects. Using an operation called binary opening, we can remove them:"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "execution_count": 2,
   "metadata": {},
   "outputs": [
    {
     "name": "stdout",
     "output_type": "stream",
     "text": [
      "Number of objects: 48\n"
     ]
    },
    {
     "data": {
      "image/png": "\n",
      "text/plain": [
       "<Figure size 720x720 with 2 Axes>"
      ]
     },
     "metadata": {
      "needs_background": "light"
     },
     "output_type": "display_data"
    }
   ],
   "source": [
    "import matplotlib.pyplot as plt\n",
    "from PIL import Image\n",
    "from numpy import *\n",
    "from scipy.ndimage import label,morphology,binary_opening\n",
    "# morphology - opening to separate objects better\n",
    "im_open = binary_opening(im,ones((9,5)),iterations=2)\n",
    "labels_open, nbr_objects_open = label(im_open)\n",
    "print(\"Number of objects:\", nbr_objects_open)\n",
    "# Figure\n",
    "fig, axs = plt.subplots(1, 2,figsize=(10,10))\n",
    "axs[0].imshow(im_open,cmap='gray')\n",
    "axs[0].set_title('Opened Image', size=16)\n",
    "axs[1].imshow(labels_open,cmap='gray')\n",
    "axs[1].set_title('Labels array', size=16)\n",
    "plt.tight_layout()"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "The second argument of binary_opening() specifies the structuring element, an array that indicates what neighbors to use when centered around a pixel. In this case, we used 9 pixels (4 above, the pixel itself, and 4 below) in the y direction and 5 in the x direction. You can specify any array as structuring element; the non-zero elements will determine the neighbors. The parameter iterations determines how many times to apply the operation. Try this and see how the number of objects changes. The image after opening and the corresponding label image are shown in the above figure. As you\n",
    "might expect, there is a function named binary_closing() that does the reverse. We leave that and the other functions in morphology and measurements to the exercises. You can learnmore about them fromthe scipy.ndimage documentation http://docs.scipy.org/doc/scipy/reference/ndimage.html."
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "***An advanced example - Image de-noising***\n",
    "\n",
    "Image denoising is the process of removing image noise while at the same time trying to preserve details and structures. We will use the Rudin-Osher-Fatemi de-noising model (ROF). Removing noise from images is important for many applications, from making your holiday photos look better to improving the quality of satellite images. The ROF model has the interesting property that it finds a smoother version of the image while preserving edges and structures. \n",
    "\n",
    "The underlying mathematics of the ROF model and the solution techniques are quite advanced and outside the scope of this book. We’ll give a brief, simplified introduction before showing how to implement a ROF solver based on an algorithm by Chambolle.\n",
    "\n",
    "The total variation (TV) of a (grayscale) image $I$ can be defined as the sum of the gradient norm. In a continuous representation, this is \n",
    "$$J \\left( I \\right) = \\int \\left| \\nabla I \\right| \\ \\mathrm{d} \\mathbf{x}$$\n",
    "and in a discrete setting the integral is replaced by a sum over all image co-ordinates $\\mathbf{x} = [x,y]$, i.e.,\n",
    "$$J \\left( I \\right) = \\sum_{\\mathbf{x}} \\left| \\nabla I \\right| \\ .$$\n",
    "According to Solem, in the Chambolle version of ROF, the goal is to find a de-noised image $U$ that solves the optimization problem\n",
    "$$\\min_{U} \\left\\| I - U \\right\\|^2 + 2 \\lambda J \\left( U \\right) \\ ,$$\n",
    "Note that the norm $\\left\\| I - U \\right\\|$ measures the difference between the de-noised image $U$ and the original image $I$ and the second term describes the total variation of $U$. This makes the solution both close to the original image as well as allowing for discontinuities, i.e., sharp corners.\n",
    "\n",
    "Comment: There are different objective functions for the minimization problem stated in Solem's book. Hence, the mathematics there should be regarded critically. For instance, in https://www.ipol.im/pub/art/2013/61/article_lr.pdf and https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Total_variation_denoising the theory is slightly different. Moreover, the minimization should be carried out over a space of functions of \"bounded variation\".   "
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "execution_count": 3,
   "metadata": {},
   "outputs": [
    {
     "data": {
      "image/png": "\n",
      "text/plain": [
       "<Figure size 432x288 with 3 Axes>"
      ]
     },
     "metadata": {
      "needs_background": "light"
     },
     "output_type": "display_data"
    }
   ],
   "source": [
    "from numpy import *\n",
    "\n",
    "def denoise(im,U_init,tolerance=0.1,tau=0.125,tv_weight=100):\n",
    "    \"\"\" An implementation of the Rudin-Osher-Fatemi (ROF) denoising model\n",
    "        using the numerical procedure presented in Eq. (11) of A. Chambolle\n",
    "        (2005). Implemented using periodic boundary conditions.\n",
    "        \n",
    "        Input: noisy input image (grayscale), initial guess for U, weight of \n",
    "        the TV-regularizing term, steplength, tolerance for the stop criterion\n",
    "        \n",
    "        Output: denoised and detextured image, texture residual. \"\"\"\n",
    "        \n",
    "    m,n = im.shape #size of noisy image\n",
    "\n",
    "    # initialize\n",
    "    U = U_init\n",
    "    Px = zeros((m, n)) #x-component to the dual field\n",
    "    Py = zeros((m, n)) #y-component of the dual field\n",
    "    error = 1 \n",
    "    \n",
    "    while (error > tolerance):\n",
    "        Uold = U\n",
    "        \n",
    "        # gradient of primal variable\n",
    "        GradUx = roll(U,-1,axis=1)-U # x-component of U's gradient\n",
    "        GradUy = roll(U,-1,axis=0)-U # y-component of U's gradient\n",
    "        \n",
    "        # update the dual varible\n",
    "        PxNew = Px + (tau/tv_weight)*GradUx # non-normalized update of x-component (dual)\n",
    "        PyNew = Py + (tau/tv_weight)*GradUy # non-normalized update of y-component (dual)\n",
    "        NormNew = maximum(1,sqrt(PxNew**2+PyNew**2))\n",
    "        \n",
    "        Px = PxNew/NormNew # update of x-component (dual)\n",
    "        Py = PyNew/NormNew # update of y-component (dual)\n",
    "        \n",
    "        # update the primal variable\n",
    "        RxPx = roll(Px,1,axis=1) # right x-translation of x-component\n",
    "        RyPy = roll(Py,1,axis=0) # right y-translation of y-component\n",
    "        \n",
    "        DivP = (Px-RxPx)+(Py-RyPy) # divergence of the dual field.\n",
    "        U = im + tv_weight*DivP # update of the primal variable\n",
    "        \n",
    "        # update of error\n",
    "        error = linalg.norm(U-Uold)/sqrt(n*m);\n",
    "        \n",
    "    return U,im-U # denoised image and texture residual\n",
    "\n",
    "\n",
    "from pylab import *\n",
    "from numpy import *\n",
    "from numpy import random\n",
    "from scipy.ndimage import gaussian_filter\n",
    "\n",
    "\"\"\"\n",
    "This is the de-noising example using ROF\n",
    "\"\"\"\n",
    "\n",
    "# create synthetic image with noise\n",
    "im = zeros((500,500))\n",
    "im[100:400,100:400] = 128\n",
    "im[200:300,200:300] = 255\n",
    "im = im + 30*random.standard_normal((500,500))\n",
    "\n",
    "U,T = denoise(im,im)\n",
    "G = gaussian_filter(im,10)\n",
    "\n",
    "\n",
    "# save the result\n",
    "imsave('synth_original.pdf',im)\n",
    "imsave('synth_rof.pdf',U)\n",
    "imsave('synth_gaussian.pdf',G)\n",
    "\n",
    "\n",
    "# plot\n",
    "figure()\n",
    "gray()\n",
    "\n",
    "subplot(1,3,1)\n",
    "imshow(im)\n",
    "axis('equal')\n",
    "axis('off')\n",
    "\n",
    "subplot(1,3,2)\n",
    "imshow(G)\n",
    "axis('equal')\n",
    "axis('off')\n",
    "\n",
    "subplot(1,3,3)\n",
    "imshow(U)\n",
    "axis('equal')\n",
    "axis('off')\n",
    "\n",
    "show()"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "In this example, we used the function roll(), which, as the name suggests, “rolls” the values of an array cyclically around an axis. This is very convenient for computing neighbor differences, in this case for derivatives. We also used linalg.norm(), which measures the difference between two arrays (in this case, the image matrices U and Uold)."
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "***Local image descriptors (features)***\n",
    "\n",
    "Now we will look into finding corresponding points and regions between images. Two different types of local descriptors are introduced with methods for matching these between images.These local featureswill be used in many different contexts throughout this book and are an important building block in many applications, such as creating panoramas, augmented reality, and computing 3D reconstructions.\n",
    "\n",
    "*The Harris corner detector*\n",
    "\n",
    "This is one of the simplest corner indicators available. The general idea is to locate interest points where the surrounding neighborhood shows edges in more than one direction; these are then image corners. We define a matrix $\\mathbf{M}_I = \\mathbf{M}_I \\left( \\mathbf{x} \\right)$, on the points $\\mathbf{x}$ in the image domain, as the positive semi-definite, symmetric matrix\n",
    "$$\\mathbf{M}_I = \\nabla I \\ \\nabla I^{\\mathrm{T}} = \\left( \\begin{array}{c} I_x \\\\ I_y \\end{array} \\right) \\left( \\begin{array}{cc} I_x & I_y \\end{array} \\right) = \\left( \\begin{array}{cc} I_x^2 & I_x I_y \\\\ I_x I_y & I_y^2  \\end{array} \\right) \\ ,$$\n",
    "as before $\\nabla I$ is the image gradient containing the derivatives $I_x$ and $I_y$ (we defined the derivatives and the gradient previously). Because of this construction, $\\mathbf{M}_I$ has rank one with eigenvalues $\\lambda_1 = \\left| \\nabla I \\right|^2$ and $\\lambda_2 = 0$. We now have one matrix for each pixel in the image.\n",
    "\n",
    "Let $\\mathbf{W}$ be a weight matrix (typically a Gaussian filter $G_{\\sigma}$ ). The component-wise convolution\n",
    "$$\\overline{\\mathbf{M}_I} = \\mathbf{W} * \\mathbf{M}_I$$\n",
    "gives a local averaging of $\\mathbf{M}_I$ over the neighboring pixels. The resulting matrix $\\overline{\\mathbf{M}_I}$ is sometimes called a Harris matrix. The width of $\\mathbf{W}$ determines a region of interest around $\\mathbf{x}$. The idea of averaging the matrix $\\mathbf{M}_I$ over a region like this is that the eigenvalues will change depending on the local image properties. If the gradients vary in the region, the second eigenvalue of $\\overline{\\mathbf{M}_I}$ will no longer be zero. If the gradients are the same, the eigenvalues will be the same as for $\\mathbf{M}_I$.\n",
    "\n",
    "Depending on the values of $\\nabla I$ in the region, there are three cases for the eigenvalues of the Harris matrix, $\\overline{\\mathbf{M}_I}$:\n",
    "- If $\\lambda_1$ and $\\lambda_2$ both have large positive values, then there is a corner at $\\mathbf{x}$.\n",
    "- If $\\lambda_1$ is large and $\\lambda_2 \\approx 0$, then there is an edge and the averaging of $\\mathbf{M}_I$ over the\n",
    "region doesn’t change the eigenvalues that much.\n",
    "- If $\\lambda_1 \\approx \\lambda_2 \\approx 0$, then there is nothing.\n",
    "To distinguish the important case from the others without actually having to compute the eigenvalues, Harris and Stephens introduced an indicator function\n",
    "$$\\det{\\left( \\overline{\\mathbf{M}_I} \\right)} - \\kappa \\ \\mathrm{trace}{\\left( \\overline{\\mathbf{M}_I} \\right)}^2 \\ .$$\n",
    "Although, to get rid of the weighting constant $\\kappa$ it is often more practical to use the following indicator function:\n",
    "$$\\frac{ \\det{\\left( \\overline{\\mathbf{M}_I} \\right)} }{ \\mathrm{trace}{\\left( \\overline{\\mathbf{M}_I} \\right)}^2 }$$\n",
    "\n",
    "Let’s see what this looks like in code. For this, we need the scipy.ndimage.filters module for computing derivatives using Gaussian derivative filters as described previously. The reason is again that we would like to suppress noise sensitivity in the corner detection process. First, add the corner response function to a file harris.py, which will make use of the Gaussian derivatives. Again, the parameter $\\sigma$ defines the scale of the Gaussian filters used. You can also modify this function to take different scales in the $x$ and $y$ directions, as well as a different scale for the averaging, to compute the Harris matrix."
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "execution_count": 4,
   "metadata": {},
   "outputs": [],
   "source": [
    "from pylab import *\n",
    "from numpy import *\n",
    "from scipy.ndimage import filters\n",
    "\n",
    "def compute_harris_response(im,sigma=3):\n",
    "    \"\"\" Compute the Harris corner detector response function \n",
    "        for each pixel in a graylevel image. \"\"\"\n",
    "    \n",
    "    # derivatives\n",
    "    imx = zeros(im.shape)\n",
    "    gaussian_filter(im, (sigma,sigma), (0,1), imx)\n",
    "    imy = zeros(im.shape)\n",
    "    gaussian_filter(im, (sigma,sigma), (1,0), imy)\n",
    "    \n",
    "    # compute components of the Harris matrix\n",
    "    Wxx = gaussian_filter(imx*imx,sigma)\n",
    "    Wxy = gaussian_filter(imx*imy,sigma)\n",
    "    Wyy = gaussian_filter(imy*imy,sigma)\n",
    "    \n",
    "    # determinant and trace\n",
    "    Wdet = Wxx*Wyy - Wxy**2\n",
    "    Wtr = Wxx + Wyy\n",
    "    \n",
    "    return Wdet / Wtr"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "This gives an image with each pixel containing the value of the Harris response function. Now, it is just a matter of picking out the information needed from this image. Taking all points with values above a threshold, with the additional constraint that corners must be separated with a minimum distance, is an approach that often gives good results. To do this, take all candidate pixels, sort them in descending order of corner response values, and mark off regions too close to positions already marked as corners. Add the following function:"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "execution_count": 5,
   "metadata": {},
   "outputs": [],
   "source": [
    "def get_harris_points(harrisim,min_dist=10,threshold=0.1):\n",
    "    \"\"\" Return corners from a Harris response image\n",
    "        min_dist is the minimum number of pixels separating \n",
    "        corners and image boundary. \"\"\"\n",
    "    \n",
    "    # find top corner candidates above a threshold\n",
    "    corner_threshold = harrisim.max() * threshold\n",
    "    harrisim_t = (harrisim > corner_threshold) * 1\n",
    "    \n",
    "    # get coordinates of candidates\n",
    "    coords = array(harrisim_t.nonzero()).T\n",
    "    \n",
    "    # ...and their values\n",
    "    candidate_values = [harrisim[c[0],c[1]] for c in coords]\n",
    "    \n",
    "    # sort candidates (reverse to get descending order)\n",
    "    index = argsort(candidate_values)[::-1]\n",
    "    \n",
    "    # store allowed point locations in array\n",
    "    allowed_locations = zeros(harrisim.shape)\n",
    "    allowed_locations[min_dist:-min_dist,min_dist:-min_dist] = 1\n",
    "    \n",
    "    # select the best points taking min_distance into account\n",
    "    filtered_coords = []\n",
    "    for i in index:\n",
    "        if allowed_locations[coords[i,0],coords[i,1]] == 1:\n",
    "            filtered_coords.append(coords[i])\n",
    "            allowed_locations[(coords[i,0]-min_dist):(coords[i,0]+min_dist), \n",
    "                        (coords[i,1]-min_dist):(coords[i,1]+min_dist)] = 0\n",
    "    \n",
    "    return filtered_coords"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "Now we have all we need to detect corner points in images. To show the corner points in the image, add a plotting function using Matplotlib as follows:"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "execution_count": 6,
   "metadata": {},
   "outputs": [],
   "source": [
    "def plot_harris_points(image,filtered_coords):\n",
    "    \"\"\" Plots corners found in image. \"\"\"\n",
    "    \n",
    "    figure()\n",
    "    gray()\n",
    "    imshow(image)\n",
    "    plot([p[1] for p in filtered_coords],\n",
    "                [p[0] for p in filtered_coords],'*')\n",
    "    axis('off')\n",
    "    show()"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "Run the following commands:"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "execution_count": 7,
   "metadata": {},
   "outputs": [
    {
     "data": {
      "image/png": "\n",
      "text/plain": [
       "<Figure size 432x288 with 1 Axes>"
      ]
     },
     "metadata": {
      "needs_background": "light"
     },
     "output_type": "display_data"
    }
   ],
   "source": [
    "im = array(Image.open('empire.jpg').convert('L'))\n",
    "harrisim = compute_harris_response(im)\n",
    "filtered_coords = get_harris_points(harrisim,20)\n",
    "plot_harris_points(im, filtered_coords)"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "The image is opened and converted to grayscale. Then, the response function is computed and points selected based on the response values. Finally, the points are plotted overlaid on the original image.\n",
    "\n",
    "For an overview of different approaches to corner detection, including improvements on the Harris detector and further developments, see for example http://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Corner_detection.\n",
    "\n",
    "*Finding corresponding points between images*\n",
    "\n",
    "The Harris corner detector gives interest points in images but does not contain an inherent way of comparing these interest points across images to find matching corners. What we need is to add a descriptor to each point and a way to compare such\n",
    "descriptors.\n",
    "\n",
    "An *interest point descriptor* is a vector assigned to an interest point that describes the image appearance around the point. The better the descriptor, the better your correspondences will be. By point correspondence or corresponding points, we mean points in different images that refer to the same object or scene point. \n",
    "\n",
    "Harris corner points are usually combined with a descriptor consisting of the graylevel values in a neighboring image patch together with normalized cross-correlation for comparison. An *image patch* is almost always a rectangular portion of the image centered around the point in question.\n",
    "\n",
    "In general, the *correlation* between two (equally sized) image patches $I_1(\\mathbf{x})$ and $I_2(\\mathbf{x})$ is\n",
    "defined as\n",
    "\n",
    "$$c \\left( I_1, I_2 \\right) = \\sum_{\\mathbf{x}} f \\left( I_1(\\mathbf{x}) , I_2(\\mathbf{x}) \\right) \\ ,$$\n",
    "\n",
    "where the function $f$ varies depending on the correlation method. The sum is taken over all positions $\\mathbf{x}$ in the image patches. For *cross-correlation*, the function is $f \\left( I_1(\\mathbf{x}) , I_2(\\mathbf{x}) \\right) = I_1 \\ I_2$, making $c \\left( I_1, I_2 \\right) = I_1 \\cdot I_2$, i.e., the scalar product (of the row- or column-stacked patches). The larger the value of $c \\left( I_1, I_2 \\right)$, the more similar the patches $I_1$ and $I_2$ are.\n",
    "\n",
    "*Normalized* cross-correlation is a variant of cross-correlation defined as\n",
    "\n",
    "$$\\mathrm{ncc} \\left( I_1, I_2 \\right) = \\frac{1}{n-1} \\sum_{\\mathbf{x}} \\left[ \\frac{I_1(\\mathbf{x})-\\mu_1}{\\sigma_1} \\right] \\cdot \\left[ \\frac{I_2(\\mathbf{x})-\\mu_2}{\\sigma_2} \\right] \\ ,$$\n",
    "\n",
    "where $n$ is the number of pixels in a patch, $\\mu_1$ and $\\mu_2$ are the mean intensities, and $\\sigma_1$ and $\\sigma_2$ are the standard deviations in each patch, respectively. By subtracting the mean and scaling with the standard deviation, the method becomes robust to changes in image brightness.\n",
    "\n",
    "To extract image patches and compare them using normalized cross-correlation, we need the following functions:"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "execution_count": 8,
   "metadata": {},
   "outputs": [],
   "source": [
    "def get_descriptors(image,filtered_coords,wid=5):\n",
    "    \"\"\" For each point return pixel values around the point\n",
    "        using a neighbourhood of width 2*wid+1. (Assume points are \n",
    "        extracted with min_distance > wid). \"\"\"\n",
    "    \n",
    "    desc = []\n",
    "    for coords in filtered_coords:\n",
    "        patch = image[coords[0]-wid:coords[0]+wid+1,\n",
    "                            coords[1]-wid:coords[1]+wid+1].flatten()\n",
    "        desc.append(patch)\n",
    "    \n",
    "    return desc\n",
    "\n",
    "\n",
    "def match(desc1,desc2,threshold=0.5):\n",
    "    \"\"\" For each corner point descriptor in the first image, \n",
    "        select its match to second image using\n",
    "        normalized cross correlation. \"\"\"\n",
    "    \n",
    "    n = len(desc1[0])\n",
    "    \n",
    "    # pair-wise distances\n",
    "    d = -ones((len(desc1),len(desc2)))\n",
    "    for i in range(len(desc1)):\n",
    "        for j in range(len(desc2)):\n",
    "            d1 = (desc1[i] - mean(desc1[i])) / std(desc1[i])\n",
    "            d2 = (desc2[j] - mean(desc2[j])) / std(desc2[j])\n",
    "            ncc_value = sum(d1 * d2) / (n-1) \n",
    "            if ncc_value > threshold:\n",
    "                d[i,j] = ncc_value\n",
    "            \n",
    "    ndx = argsort(-d)\n",
    "    matchscores = ndx[:,0]\n",
    "    \n",
    "    return matchscores"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "The first function takes a square grayscale patch of odd side length centered around the point, flattens it, and adds to a list of descriptors. The second function matches each descriptor to its best candidate in the other image using normalized cross-correlation. Note that the distances are negated before sorting, since a high value means better match. To further stabilize the matches, we can match from the second image to the first and filter out the matches that are not the best both ways. The following function does just that:"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "execution_count": 9,
   "metadata": {},
   "outputs": [],
   "source": [
    "def match_twosided(desc1,desc2,threshold=0.5):\n",
    "    \"\"\" Two-sided symmetric version of match(). \"\"\"\n",
    "    \n",
    "    matches_12 = match(desc1,desc2,threshold)\n",
    "    matches_21 = match(desc2,desc1,threshold)\n",
    "    \n",
    "    ndx_12 = where(matches_12 >= 0)[0]\n",
    "    \n",
    "    # remove matches that are not symmetric\n",
    "    for n in ndx_12:\n",
    "        if matches_21[matches_12[n]] != n:\n",
    "            matches_12[n] = -1\n",
    "    \n",
    "    return matches_12"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "The matches can be visualized by showing the images side-by-side and connecting matched points with lines using the following code."
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "execution_count": 10,
   "metadata": {},
   "outputs": [],
   "source": [
    "def appendimages(im1,im2):\n",
    "    \"\"\" Return a new image that appends the two images side-by-side. \"\"\"\n",
    "    \n",
    "    # select the image with the fewest rows and fill in enough empty rows\n",
    "    rows1 = im1.shape[0]    \n",
    "    rows2 = im2.shape[0]\n",
    "    \n",
    "    if rows1 < rows2:\n",
    "        im1 = concatenate((im1,zeros((rows2-rows1,im1.shape[1]))),axis=0)\n",
    "    elif rows1 > rows2:\n",
    "        im2 = concatenate((im2,zeros((rows1-rows2,im2.shape[1]))),axis=0)\n",
    "    # if none of these cases they are equal, no filling needed.\n",
    "    \n",
    "    return concatenate((im1,im2), axis=1)\n",
    "    \n",
    "    \n",
    "def plot_matches(im1,im2,locs1,locs2,matchscores,show_below=True):\n",
    "    \"\"\" Show a figure with lines joining the accepted matches \n",
    "        input: im1,im2 (images as arrays), locs1,locs2 (feature locations), \n",
    "        matchscores (as output from 'match()'), \n",
    "        show_below (if images should be shown below matches). \"\"\"\n",
    "    \n",
    "    im3 = appendimages(im1,im2)\n",
    "    if show_below:\n",
    "        im3 = vstack((im3,im3))\n",
    "    \n",
    "    imshow(im3)\n",
    "    \n",
    "    cols1 = im1.shape[1]\n",
    "    for i,m in enumerate(matchscores):\n",
    "        if m>0:\n",
    "            plot([locs1[i][1],locs2[m][1]+cols1],[locs1[i][0],locs2[m][0]],'c')\n",
    "    axis('off')"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "We can now try to find such corresponding points using normalized cross-correlation (in this case, with $11 \\times 11$ pixels in a patch) using the following commands:"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "code",
   "execution_count": 11,
   "metadata": {},
   "outputs": [
    {
     "name": "stdout",
     "output_type": "stream",
     "text": [
      "starting matching\n"
     ]
    },
    {
     "data": {
      "image/png": "\n",
      "text/plain": [
       "<Figure size 432x288 with 1 Axes>"
      ]
     },
     "metadata": {
      "needs_background": "light"
     },
     "output_type": "display_data"
    }
   ],
   "source": [
    "import os\n",
    "from pylab import *\n",
    "from numpy import *\n",
    "from PIL import Image\n",
    "\n",
    "def imresize(im,sz):\n",
    "    \"\"\"    Resize an image array using PIL. \"\"\"\n",
    "    pil_im = Image.fromarray(uint8(im))\n",
    "    \n",
    "    return array(pil_im.resize(sz))\n",
    "\n",
    "im1 = array(Image.open(\"crans_1_small.jpg\").convert(\"L\"))\n",
    "im2 = array(Image.open(\"crans_2_small.jpg\").convert(\"L\"))\n",
    "\n",
    "# resize to make matching faster\n",
    "# im1 = imresize(im1,(im1.shape[1]/2,im1.shape[0]/2))\n",
    "# im2 = imresize(im2,(im2.shape[1]/2,im2.shape[0]/2))\n",
    "\n",
    "wid = 5\n",
    "harrisim = compute_harris_response(im1,5)\n",
    "filtered_coords1 = get_harris_points(harrisim,wid+1)\n",
    "d1 = get_descriptors(im1,filtered_coords1,wid)\n",
    "harrisim = compute_harris_response(im2,5)\n",
    "filtered_coords2 = get_harris_points(harrisim,wid+1)\n",
    "d2 = get_descriptors(im2,filtered_coords2,wid)\n",
    "print('starting matching')\n",
    "matches = match_twosided(d1,d2)\n",
    "figure()\n",
    "gray()\n",
    "plot_matches(im1,im2,filtered_coords1,filtered_coords2,matches)\n",
    "show()"
   ]
  },
  {
   "cell_type": "markdown",
   "metadata": {},
   "source": [
    "If you only want to plot a subset of the matches to make the visualization clearer, substitute *matches* with, for example, *matches[:100]* or a random set of indices.\n",
    "\n",
    "As you can see, there are quite a lot of incorrect matches. This is because cross-correlation on image patches is not as descriptive as more modern approaches. As a consequence, it is important to use robust methods for handling these correspondences in an application. Another problem is that these descriptors are not invariant to scale or rotation, and the choice of patch sizes affects the results."
   ]
  }
 ],
